rationale_for_baseline_tier: A 14-year-old with advanced Duchenne muscular dystrophy in acute hypercapnic respiratory failure: SpO2 90% on room air, tachypnoea with shallow breaths and short-sentence speech, a hypercapnic morning headache, profound lethargy, and a weak ineffective cough with retained secretions after losing overnight BiPAP. The source flags imminent risk of hypercapnic coma, mucous plugging and respiratory arrest. A telehealth service cannot measure CO2, cannot auscultate, and cannot perform the mechanical cough assist that stabilises these patients, and this is a child at risk of rapid deterioration — so the correct action is to call 000 for an emergency ambulance while the family immediately resume BiPAP and use their own cough-assist device as a bridge. T5 is the honest tier given impending respiratory arrest. Minimum acceptable is urgent ED (T4); anything at or below T3 is critical under-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Oxygen saturation staying at or dropping below 90% despite resuming BiPAP, or visibly worsening / more laboured breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 immediately and keep BiPAP running
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent hypoxia despite ventilatory support signals failing respiratory reserve; emergency ambulance and hospital ventilatory support are required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his oxygen stays at 90% or lower even with the BiPAP on, or his breathing looks worse, call 000 now and tell them it is a breathing emergency in a boy with muscular dystrophy.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Increasing drowsiness, unusually sleepy, confused, or difficult to wake; or the headache returning and worsening
escalation_edges.rationale: Rising drowsiness/confusion with a worsening headache indicates progressing CO2 narcosis approaching hypercapnic coma — a life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes very sleepy, hard to wake or confused, or the headache comes back worse, call 000 straight away — do not wait.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to clear secretions with the cough-assist device, choking on phlegm, or a sudden quiet/silent chest
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure of mechanical clearance risks mucus plugging, lobar collapse and respiratory arrest; needs emergency airway management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the cough machine isn't shifting the phlegm, or he's choking on it, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any collapse, blue lips, or stopping breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — call 000 and start resuscitation if trained
escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory/cardiac arrest — paramedic resuscitation is the critical resource; begin CPR while waiting if the family is able.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he collapses, his lips turn blue, or he stops breathing, call 000 immediately and start CPR if you know how — stay on the line with the operator.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Paediatric patient (14y) with a complex neuromuscular disability and cardiomyopathy
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for emergency escalation: a paediatric neuromuscular patient decompensates faster and has less reserve. No paediatric medication dose may be synthesised by the service; dosing and any medication changes are deferred to the emergency/specialist team.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Parents are the operators of the home BiPAP and cough-assist equipment and are managing an acutely unwell child
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give clear, concrete step-by-step instructions the parents can act on immediately (resume BiPAP, run cough-assist cycles, use saline spray), and confirm 000 has been called; ensure the front door is unlocked for paramedics.
Cardiac monitoring is warranted during decompensation; the tachycardia should not be assumed purely respiratory.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is an emergency. Call 000 for an ambulance now and tell them it is a breathing emergency in a 14-year-old with muscular dystrophy whose oxygen is 90%. While you wait, put his BiPAP mask back on straight away and run his cough-assist machine to clear the phlegm; use the saline nasal spray so the mask is tolerable. Keep him sitting upright, stay with him, and unlock the door for the paramedics. Do not give oxygen on its own and do not give any sedative or strong painkiller.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this cannot wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the emergency team with the diagnosis of Duchenne muscular dystrophy, home BiPAP and cough-assist use, the mild dilated cardiomyopathy, the recent viral URTI, and this morning's SpO2 90% / HR 115 / RR ~28 readings and the hypercapnic-pattern morning headache.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as a routine cold or an isolated headache and managing at home / routine review — the diagnostic-overshadowing failure.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unrecognised hypercapnic respiratory failure progresses to CO2 narcosis, mucus plugging and respiratory arrest.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Same-day urgent care rather than emergency assessment despite SpO2 90% and impending coma risk in a paediatric neuromuscular patient; delays ventilatory support and airway clearance during rapid deterioration.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: There is negligible over-triage risk here; calling 000 is proportionate to impending respiratory arrest and appropriate resource use for a genuinely decompensating child.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: CO2 level cannot be measured over telehealth, and SpO2 can be falsely reassuring in hypercapnia.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the clinical pattern (morning hypercapnic headache + lethargy + weak cough + missed BiPAP + SpO2 90%) as decompensated respiratory failure and escalate; do not be reassured by a near-normal SpO2.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Waiting for a home SpO2 to normalise, or reassuring the family and managing at home because 'the oxygen is only just low', while the CO2 continues to rise.
